draft.introduction: The Hamðismál (“Lay of Hamðir”) is the final poem of the Codex Regius Poetic Edda, narrating the doomed vengeance raid of Guðrún’s sons against King Jǫrmunrekkr. It is a poem of fatalistic heroism where the heroes know they will die.

translation.text: We have fought well; we stand on Gothic corpses, on the sword-weary, like eagles on a branch. We have won good fame, though we die now or tomorrow. No man outlasts the evening after the Norns have spoken.
translation.original_text: Vel hǫfum vegit, stǫndum á val-Gotna ofan eggmóðum, sem ernir á kvisti. Góðs hǫfum tir of fengit; kvǫlld lifir maðr ekki ept kvið norna.

translator_notes.note: The maxim “no man outlasts the evening after the Norns have spoken” encapsulates the Norse fatalistic worldview. Unlike the Greek Moirai, the Norns’ decrees cannot be appealed even by the gods—at Ragnarǫk, Odin himself falls according to fate.
